title: The Nature of the Firm
main_point: Firms exist because internal coordination saves on transaction costs relative to the market price mechanism; firm boundaries set where marginal internal cost equals marginal market cost.

title: Vertical Integration, Appropriate Rents, and the Competitive Contracting Process
main_point: Relationship-specific assets create quasi-rents and holdup risk; vertical integration or long-term contracting emerges to protect against opportunistic appropriation.

title: Corporate Finance, the Theory of the Firm, and Organizations
main_point: Survey linking incomplete-contracts theory of the firm to corporate finance: control rights, financial contracts, and organizational boundaries jointly determined by information and enforcement frictions.

title: In Search of New Foundations
main_point: Traditional property-rights view of the firm needs rethinking as human capital and relationships become the key assets; calls for foundations built on critical resources rather than physical asset ownership.

title: The Use of Knowledge in Society
main_point: Knowledge is dispersed and local; the price system aggregates it efficiently, so central planning cannot replicate market outcomes. Foundational for market- vs-hierarchy debate.

title: Uncertainty, Evolution and Economic Theory
main_point: Under uncertainty, firms need not maximize profit consciously; market selection rewards surviving behavior so realized outcomes look as if firms optimized. Evolutionary foundation for positive economics.

title: The Methodology of Positive Economics
main_point: Theories should be judged by predictive accuracy of their implications, not realism of assumptions. Justifies abstract firm models (incl. profit maximization) as instrumental.

title: The Real Effects of Hedge Fund Activism
main_point: Plant-level evidence that hedge-fund activism causes productivity gains at target firms' plants; operational rather than purely financial value creation.

title: Value creation in shareholder activism
main_point: Structural model of activism: value gains come from information acquisition + engagement, not just stock picking; most returns are real value creation, not tipping.

title: Anticompetitive Effects of Common Ownership
main_point: In US airline markets, higher common institutional ownership across rivals correlates with higher prices; suggests common owners dampen competition.

title: Does common ownership really increase firm coordination?
main_point: Re-examines Azar-Schmalz-Tecu channels; finds little evidence that common owners actively coordinate firms' strategies, casting doubt on behavioral mechanism.

title: The costs of intense board monitoring
main_point: Boards with majority of directors on monitoring committees over-monitor: lower R&D, acquisitions, and firm value for innovative firms. Monitoring has costs.

title: The changing of the boards: The impact on firm value of mandated female board representation
main_point: Norwegian 40% female board quota caused sharp Tobin's Q decline at mandated firms, driven by younger/less-experienced female appointees.

title: Valuation effects of Norway's board gender-quota law revisited
main_point: Re-examines AD 2012 with cleaner identification; finds little negative value effect of Norway's quota, reversing earlier conclusions.

title: Independent Director Incentives: Where Do Talented Directors Spend Their Limited Time and Energy?
main_point: Talented outside directors with multiple board seats prioritize larger/more prestigious boards in allocating effort; attendance and effort track incentives.

title: Selecting Directors Using Machine Learning
main_point: An ML model trained on director characteristics predicts which nominees will underperform; suggests real boards make systematic selection mistakes correctable by data.

title: Managerial risk-taking behavior and equity-based compensation
main_point: Following Delaware takeover-friendly case law, CEOs reduced firm risk (stock-return vol, leverage). Managerial risk aversion matters; compensation convexity helps align.

title: Stock options and managerial incentives for risk taking: Evidence from FAS 123R
main_point: After FAS 123R forced expensing of options, firms cut option pay sharply, but no measurable change in risk-taking, challenging convexity-risk-taking linkage.

title: Duration of Executive Compensation
main_point: Constructs duration measure for CEO pay (vesting horizons); longer duration correlates with longer-horizon investment (R&D, capex) and less earnings manipulation.

title: What Doesn't Kill You Only Makes You More Risk-Loving: Early Life Disasters and CEO Behavior
main_point: CEOs exposed as children to moderate natural disasters take more risk as adults; extreme-disaster exposure reduces risk-taking. U-shaped formative-experience effect.

title: The market for corporate assets: who engages in mergers and asset sales and are there efficiency gains?
main_point: Plant-level data: asset sales move assets to more-productive users; efficiency gains real, consistent with neoclassical reallocation theory.

title: What drives merger waves?
main_point: Waves driven by industry shocks + capital-liquidity conditions, not primarily behavioral mis-valuation; neoclassical drivers dominate.

title: Valuation waves and merger activity: The empirical evidence
main_point: Decomposes M/B into firm-specific mispricing, sector-wide mispricing, and long-run value; stock-financed mergers cluster at high sector-mispricing periods.

title: Determinants of cross-border mergers
main_point: Cross-border M&A driven by valuation gaps, exchange-rate movements, and geographic/cultural/legal distance; acquirer-target country characteristics matter.

title: Product market synergies and competition in mergers and acquisitions: A text-based analysis
main_point: 10-K text similarity between acquirer and target predicts deal outcomes: synergy gains higher, competition concerns higher when product descriptions overlap.

title: The importance of industry links in merger waves
main_point: Industry merger waves propagate along input-output (customer-supplier) linkages; industries connected to a wave-industry experience their own waves.

title: Inefficiencies and externalities from opportunistic acquirers
main_point: Acquirers exploit target undervaluation; deals generate negative externalities for target peers via signaling. Welfare-reducing opportunism.

title: Stealth acquisitions and product market competition
main_point: Small, sub-reporting-threshold acquisitions escape antitrust review but cumulatively concentrate markets and raise prices. Enforcement gap in US merger policy.

title: Do Going-Private Transactions Affect Plant Efficiency and Investment?
main_point: After LBOs, plants see lower investment but no productivity gains; challenges efficiency-improvement story for private equity going-private deals.

title: The Operational Consequences of Private Equity Buyouts: Evidence from the Restaurant Industry
main_point: PE buyouts of restaurant chains improve operational outcomes (health inspections, food safety) at store level; PE drives real operational improvements.

title: Private equity and workers' career paths: The role of technological change
main_point: PE targets with more IT use see workers gain more IT skills, leading to better post-PE wages; PE accelerates tech adoption and human-capital upgrading.

title: Do insiders time management buyouts and freezeouts to buy undervalued targets?
main_point: Insiders execute MBOs / freeze-outs following periods of negative firm-specific news; evidence of information-based timing and wealth transfer from minority shareholders.

title: Uncertainty and investment dynamics
main_point: With adjustment costs + real options, uncertainty reduces responsiveness of investment to demand shocks; both the level and sensitivity of investment fall.

title: Intangible capital and the investment-q relation
main_point: Adding intangible capital to the q model restores the investment-q relation; intangibles explain why Tobin's q predicts investment poorly under standard measures.

title: Why did the q theory of investment start working?
main_point: Time-varying empirical fit of q theory: as measurement of true q improves (accounting, intangibles), the investment-q link strengthens post-2000.

title: Product life cycles in corporate finance
main_point: Text-based product-life-cycle stages (birth/growth/maturity/decline) drive capital structure, investment, cash policy; life-cycle is a first-order corporate-finance variable.

title: Performance persistence in entrepreneurship
main_point: Serial entrepreneurs who succeeded before succeed again more often than random; success persistence suggests real entrepreneurial skill, not luck.

title: With a little help from my (random) friends: Success and failure in post-business school entrepreneurship
main_point: HBS random-section assignment: peer exposure to entrepreneurship lowers post-graduation start-up rates among less-experienced students; peer information acts as negative screen.

title: Financing innovation: Evidence from R&D grants
main_point: RD discontinuity around DOE SBIR grant cutoffs: grant receipt doubles patents and sales for early-stage firms; financing frictions bind for small innovators.

title: Cost of experimentation and the evolution of venture capital
main_point: Lower experimentation costs (cloud/open-source) shifted VC toward smaller, staged bets; explains VC industry's shift to 'spray and pray' early-stage strategies.

title: Capital structure and debt structure
main_point: Firms use multiple debt types/tiers simultaneously (bank, bond, secured, unsecured) in systematic patterns; debt structure is a distinct capital-structure dimension beyond leverage ratio.

title: As certain as debt and taxes: Estimating the tax sensitivity of leverage from state tax changes
main_point: State corporate tax changes: leverage rises after tax hikes, but does not fall after cuts. Asymmetric adjustment consistent with frictions/leverage-ratchet.

title: Do measures of financial constraints measure financial constraints?
main_point: Four popular constraint indices (KZ, WW, HP, SA) do not track actual constraints tested via tax changes; they capture size/age effects rather than true financial frictions.

title: Leverage and cash dynamics
main_point: Firms manage leverage and cash jointly with substantial deviations; long-run dynamics show firms actively rebalance capital structure toward targets rather than behaving passively.

title: Foreign cash: Taxes, international capital markets, and agency problems
main_point: Trapped foreign cash (US repatriation tax) is valued less than domestic cash; weaker governance firms have even lower foreign-cash valuation. Tax + agency drive the discount.

title: Financial flexibility, risk management, and payout choice
main_point: Firms facing more cash-flow risk choose flexible payouts (repurchases) over commitment payouts (dividends); hedging and flexibility substitute.

title: Precautionary savings with risky assets: When cash is not cash
main_point: Firms hold substantial risky securities in 'cash' reserves; precautionary-savings theory must account for portfolio-composition choice, not just quantity.

title: Disappearing and reappearing dividends
main_point: Documents dividend-payer population cycles: firms stopped paying (Fama-French 2001), then resumed; life-cycle and firm-quality effects drive the return.

title: Capital structure as a strategic variable: Evidence from collective bargaining
main_point: Firms in unionized industries increase leverage strategically to weaken labor's bargaining position (reducing perceived free cash); leverage as commitment device.

title: Broad-based employee stock ownership: Motives and outcomes
main_point: Small ESOPs are productivity-enhancing; large ESOPs are defensive (anti-takeover). Outcomes depend on scale: small for incentives, large for entrenchment.

title: The role of government in the labor-creditor relationship: Evidence from the Chrysler bankruptcy
main_point: Chrysler's 2009 government-sponsored bankruptcy favored labor (UAW) over senior creditors, altering bondholder-union balance; priority reversal had long-term debt-market effects.

title: Labor representation in governance as an insurance mechanism
main_point: German codetermination: worker-directors trade lower wage growth in good times for employment insurance in bad times; governance as risk-sharing mechanism.

title: Destructive creation at work: How financial distress spurs entrepreneurship
main_point: Displaced employees at distressed firms start new firms; distress-driven entrepreneurship is a channel through which creative destruction reallocates talent.

title: Arbitraging labor markets
main_point: Firms exploit labor-market frictions across regions/jurisdictions to move or hire where workers are relatively cheap; labor-market arbitrage as a real-decision margin.

title: Does corporate governance matter in competitive industries?
main_point: Governance (G-index) has measurable effects only in non-competitive industries; competition and governance are substitutes as disciplining devices.

title: Competition and the cost of debt
main_point: Loan spreads are higher for firms in more competitive product markets; banks price competitive threat as risk. Competition affects financing costs, not just revenues.
